summary: Gruber examines the viral story that Apple's iOS Weather app deliberately avoids displaying 69°F, concluding it's almost certainly a Celsius-to-Fahrenheit integer rounding artifact rather than intentional prudishness. The evidence is clear: the same rounding affects mundane temperatures like 65°F, negative 69°F displays fine, and every other Apple app shows 69 without issue. He argues that while Apple's reputation for prudishness made the theory plausible to many, deliberately falsifying a temperature reading would cross a line from prudish into dishonest. He connects this to a similar pattern where widely-cited reports claimed Apple TV+ would avoid R-rated content, which turned out to be false given shows like Servant and See.
key_points: The Weather app's missing 69°F is a Celsius-to-Fahrenheit integer conversion artifact — 20°C = 68°F and 21°C rounds to 70°F, skipping 69
Apple's reputation for prudishness made people eager to believe an implausible conspiracy, but the same bug affects boring numbers like 65
There's a meaningful distinction between Apple being prudish about App Store screenshots and Apple falsifying data in its own apps
The pattern mirrors the debunked 2018 WSJ report that Apple TV+ would avoid all risqué content, which shows like See and Servant thoroughly disproved
money_quotes: Sometimes a cigar is just an integer math conversion glitch.
But Apple's reputation for prudishness precedes it.
What didn't pass the sniff test for me with this 'won't show 69°F' idea is that it would cross the line into losing integrity, or at least losing accuracy.
Can I imagine a third-party weather app being rejected from the App Store because its screenshots show a big '69°F' current temperature? Yes.
But to program the iPhone Weather app to avoid displaying 69°F when it really is 69°F? No.
See was a show about a future world where everyone is blind and they pray to their god by masturbating. Disney+ probably wasn't bidding on that.
key_insight: Apple's reputation for prudishness is so well-established that people will believe implausible conspiracy theories over mundane technical explanations, but there's an important line between taste policing and falsifying data that Apple wouldn't cross.
